title: Chapter 38: The Wave
content: The contract is forty-seven pages. I've counted them three times.

Forty-seven pages of advances and royalty splits and option clauses and subsidiary rights and film adaptation riders and — God — a section about merchandise. My book, my story, my Jax, reduced to a brand extension pipeline with projected Q3 earnings.

The advance alone is more than I've made in four years of freelance journalism. Seven figures. The number sits in my stomach like a stone, heavy and luminous, and I keep turning it over in the dark when I should be sleeping.

Diane, my editor, has been patient. Three weeks of patience, which in publishing years is approximately a century. She's called twice, emailed five times, sent one fruit basket with a card that read *We're excited to move forward!* as if the contract were already signed, as if the sequel were already inevitable.
And maybe it is inevitable. That's what frightens me.
I pick up the phone. My hand shakes. I can feel the tremor in my fingertips, the slight vibration of a woman about to light seven figures on fire.
Diane answers on the second ring. "Maya! I was just —"
"The story is complete." My voice is steadier than my hands. "There is no sequel."
Silence. The kind that fills a room, that presses against the walls and the windows and the sternum.
"Maya, can we —"

"The book exists," I say. "It's out there. People are reading it and understanding what happened and that matters. That's the whole point. A sequel would be —" I search for the word, and it finds me like a key sliding into a lock — "exploitation."
"Exploitation is a strong word."
"It's the right one."
Another silence. Then Diane, whose voice has closed a thousand deals and negotiated ten thousand clauses, says quietly: "You're sure?"
"I'm sure."

I hang up before she can argue, before the seven figures can argue, before the part of me that still lives in a studio apartment with a leaky faucet and a roommate who thinks quinoa is a personality trait can argue.
The contract sits on my desk.
I pick it up — all forty-seven pages — and I tear it.

The sound is extraordinary. A clean, percussive rip that travels from my wrists to my shoulders, paper fiber separating fiber, the satisfying violence of destroying something that was never mine to begin with. I tear it again. And again. Confetti on the desk, on the floor, wedged between the keys of my laptop. I keep tearing until there's nothing left to tear, until my fingers ache and my breath comes fast and there's a pile of paper scraps on the desk like snow in a city that has never seen winter.
I did that. I chose that.
It's the hardest professional decision of my life. And the most morally clear.

Agent Elena Reyes reads the book for the third time on a Tuesday night, a glass of Tempranillo at her elbow and a yellow legal pad on her knee. She's not reading for pleasure — she's reading for coordinates.

Page 247. The passage about Jax's exit route. How she moved through twelve jurisdictions in forty-eight hours, each one chosen for its specific regulatory gap, its particular blindness. How she laundered the consciousness of the glitch through decentralized exchanges that existed in the margins between sovereign oversight.
Reyes underlines a line: *She chose jurisdictions like a musician chooses keys — not for where they were, but for what they could hold.*

Twelve jurisdictions. Reyes pulls up the blockchain data she's been triangulating for six weeks, the transaction patterns her forensic analyst flagged as anomalous but untraceable. She spreads the printouts across her kitchen table, red wine forgotten, and she starts cross-referencing.
Something aligns.

Not proof. She knows better than to call it proof. But direction — yes. The jurisdictions in the book match the jurisdictions in the data with a precision that feels like someone left a map folded inside a novel. Eleven of the twelve are confirmed. The twelfth is Lisbon.

Reyes puts down her pen. Stares at the map on her phone — Lisbon, Portugal, a city she's never visited, a city where the light is supposed to be extraordinary, where the past clings to the cobblestones like a memory that won't dissolve.
She books a flight. Departs Thursday.

The engine noise will be a low roar for ten hours, and she'll sit in it with her legal pad and her blockchain printouts and the growing certainty that she's not chasing a ghost — she's chasing someone who left just enough of a trail to be followed by someone patient enough to read.

What Reyes doesn't know — what she can't know — is that Jax Vance wrote that passage knowing exactly who might read it someday. The map was left on purpose. But the destination it leads to isn't where Jax is. It's where Jax was. A distinction that matters.
Mateo calls at eleven p.m., which means something is wrong.

"Maya." His voice has that particular flatness — the sound of someone trying very hard not to panic. I've heard it before. The night Vivienne disappeared. The night Cassandra called from the hospital. A specific frequency of controlled fear.
"What happened?"
"Cassandra got a visitor. At the nursing school."
My stomach drops. "What kind of visitor?"

"A woman. Professional. Blazer, the whole thing. Said she was doing a follow-up on 'the Vance case.' Asked about family connections, medical history — things that sound official until you realize no government agency sends someone to a nursing school to ask about your sister's medical history."

"It wasn't Reyes." I say it as a statement because I know Reyes's methods, and this isn't them. Reyes subpoenas. Reyes requests records through channels. Reyes does not show up at nursing schools asking questions like a door-to-door salesman.
"No," Mateo confirms. "Not Reyes. I checked. Cassandra got her card — or what she thought was a card. The name was fake. The company was fake. But the questions were real."
"Who —"
"Tom Hargrove."

The name lands in my chest like a slap. Tom Hargrove. Vivienne's husband. The man who lost his wife to a woman who was never supposed to exist, who watched his entire domestic architecture dissolve on a Tuesday afternoon when Vivienne walked out and didn't walk back. The man who presumably has been sitting in his Greenwich manor house nursing a grudge that money can't anesthetize.
"He hired a private investigator," Mateo says. "Cash, through a cutout. The PI is good — took us two weeks to trace the contract back to Tom's lawyer."
"Not looking for Jax," I say, understanding even as I'm saying it. "Looking for Vivienne."
"He wants her back." Mateo's voice is grim. "Or he wants to hurt her for leaving. With Tom, I'm not sure there's a difference anymore."

I close my eyes. Tom Hargrove — who I've never met, who exists in this story only as a wound Vivienne chose to leave behind, a man whose only crime was being the wrong life at the wrong time. He can't let go. Hiring a PI is the act of a man who lost control and can't accept that control was never his to begin with. Vivienne was never his to keep. She was only ever his to lose.
"Is Cassandra safe?"
"For now. The PI didn't get anything useful. But Maya — if Tom's looking, others can look. He's not the only one with resources. He's just the most motivated."
"I'll take care of it," I say.
I hang up and sit in the dark for three full minutes, listening to the hum of the refrigerator and the distant thrum of traffic on the 10, and then I pick up the phone again.
Harriet picks up on the first ring, which means she was waiting for me to call.
"I heard," she says, before I can speak. "Mateo called me too."
"Then you know what we need to do."
A pause. Harriet's pauses are architectural — they bear weight. "The relay is for emergencies, Maya."
"This is an emergency."
"Jax's definition of emergency and ours may not —"

"Tom Hargrove hired a PI to find Vivienne. If he finds her, he finds Jax. If he finds Jax, everything she built — the ghost accounts, the jurisdictions, all of it — gets exposed. Not just to Tom. To everyone. To Reyes." I'm speaking fast now, the words tumbling like water over rock. "This is exactly the kind of emergency the relay exists for."
Another pause. Then: "I'll send the message."

The decentralized relay is Jax's design — a chain of encrypted nodes that bounce a signal through a dozen countries before it reaches wherever Jax is. It can't be traced. It can't be intercepted. It can only be used once every seventy-two hours, and each use degrades the pathway slightly. Every message sent through the relay is a message that makes the next one harder to deliver.
Harriet sends the message: *Tom hired PI. Looking for V. Be aware.*
And then we wait.

Waiting is a physical sensation. It sits in my joints and behind my eyes and in the pit of my stomach where the seven figures used to live. Three hours of waiting, each one longer than the last, each one measured by the digital clock on my microwave and the analog clock in my head and the metronome of my own pulse.
Three hours.
Then the response arrives, routed through the relay's reverse channel, decrypted by Harriet's key, relayed to my phone as a single line of text:
*Acknowledged. The wave is rising.*
I read it seven times.
*The wave is rising.*

It sounds like poetry. It sounds like metaphor, like something Jax would say on a rooftop at 2 a.m. with a glass of something expensive and a smile that means she's three moves ahead of whatever game you think you're playing. It sounds like the kind of line I would have written into the book — atmospheric, evocative, just cryptic enough to make the reader lean in.
But I didn't write it. Jax did.
And Jax doesn't waste words.
I call Harriet back. "What does it mean? 'The wave is rising'?"

"I don't know." Her voice is careful. "But Maya — Jax built that relay with specific protocols. Every code phrase in the system corresponds to an action. 'The wave is rising' isn't a status update. It's an initiation."
"Initiation of what?"

"If I knew, I'd tell you. But I know this: Jax has been holding something in reserve. Something she designed before she disappeared, before the book, before any of us understood what she was actually building. I always assumed it was an escape route — a final door she could close behind her if everything collapsed. But if she's activating it now, in response to Tom Hargrove's PI..."
"It's not a door," I say. "It's an offensive."
"Maybe." Harriet's voice is quiet. "Or maybe it's both."
I hang up and stare at the message until the screen dims.
*The wave is rising.*

This is the reversal I didn't see coming, the move inside the move. I thought Jax was in hiding. I thought she was safe, secret, invisible inside the architecture she'd built to disappear. But Jax doesn't hide. Jax doesn't wait for the world to come find her. Jax builds systems within systems, and somewhere in the deepest layer of the glitch — beneath the ghost accounts and the jurisdiction chains and the blockchain mirrors — there's something she's been saving. Something big. Something that's been waiting for exactly this moment.
The moment when someone comes looking.
Jax Vance has one more move, and she's making it now.

I drive to the Santa Monica pier because I need to be somewhere that isn't my apartment, somewhere the walls aren't closing in with the weight of what I just declined and what I just learned and what's coming toward all of us like weather you can smell before you see it.

The pier is quiet for Los Angeles — a few late-night joggers, a couple sharing a bag of churros, a man fishing off the railing with the patience of someone who doesn't care if he catches anything. I walk to the end and lean against the rail and look out at the Pacific.

The ocean is black at this hour, a darkness so complete it could be the edge of the world. But it's not still. Even in the dark, the water moves — long, slow swells that rise and fall with the gravity of something larger than language. The salt wind hits my face and I taste it, brine and distance and the cold mineral breath of a planet that has never once cared about the stories we tell about it.

The sound of the waves is steady. Predictable. Each one rises, crests, and dissolves into foam at the shoreline, and another rises behind it, and another, an infinite relay of water that began somewhere across the world — somewhere like Lisbon, like the coast of Portugal, like the edge of a continent where Jax Vance may or may not be sitting in a room with a laptop and a plan and a code phrase that means *I'm still here, and I'm still protecting you, even if you can't see me do it.*

Two waves. The Pacific beneath me, vast and indifferent, governed by tides and moonlight and the physics of a spinning planet. And Jax's wave — precise, purposeful, governed by code and strategy and the particular ferocity of a woman who will not let the people she loves be found by men who think love is ownership.
*The wave is rising.*

I grip the railing. The metal is cold under my palms, the salt air sharp in my lungs. Somewhere in the dark water, the current is pulling toward shore, carrying with it everything that has ever happened in this ocean — ships and storms and the quiet dissolution of a million forgotten things. And somewhere in the dark network, Jax's final phase is unspooling, command by command, like a wave gathering force in deep water where no one can see it yet.

I don't know what she's building. I don't know if it will work. I don't know if protecting Vivienne from Tom Hargrove requires a small intervention or a seismic one, if the wave Jax is rising is a ripple or a tsunami.

But I know this: Jax is still in the game. Not as a fugitive running from the past, but as an architect building toward a future she designed before any of us knew there was a design. She anticipated Reyes. She anticipated Tom. She anticipated the moment when someone would come looking, and she prepared for it with the same meticulous attention she brought to everything — the glitch, the exit route, the way she loved Vivienne, like it was the most important architecture she would ever build.

The wind shifts. I taste salt and exhaust and the faint sweetness of churros from the vendor behind me. The Pacific stretches out to the horizon, flat and endless, and I think about how this same ocean connects California to wherever Jax is — the water touching both shores, the same current carrying the same salt, the same moon pulling the same tides. Distance made intimate by physics. Separation made connection by the simple fact of a shared planet.

Jax is out there. Somewhere on the other side of this water, in a country that doesn't exist on American maps, inside a ghost account that traces to nowhere, she is making her move. And the people she loves — Vivienne, Mateo, Harriet, me — we are protected by the architecture of her refusal to let us be found.
I pull my jacket tighter. The wind is cold and the pier is empty and the waves keep coming, one after another, relentless and ancient and indifferent to everything except the pull of the moon.
*The wave is rising.*
And I will wait. Because the story isn't over until Jax says it's over, and Jax Vance has never in her life let someone else decide when her story ends.

Not the IRS. Not Agent Reyes. Not Tom Hargrove and his private investigator. Not even me, with my torn-up contract and my bestselling book and all the words I will never write about a sequel that doesn't exist.

The Pacific doesn't answer. It just keeps moving, the way it always has, the way it always will. And somewhere out there, past the horizon, past the reach of subpoenas and blockchains and the wounded hunger of a man who can't let go, Jax's wave is rising — patient, purposeful, inevitable.
I stand at the rail and I watch, and I wait.
